coredata.dc:title: A simple fluorometric method for rapid screening of aflatoxins after their extraction by magnetic MOF-808/graphene oxide composite and their discrimination by HPLC
coredata.dc:creator: Alilou, Sevda
Amirzehni, Maliheh
Eslami, Parvin Alizadeh
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a high-performance screening tool for the selective and sensitive monitoring of aflatoxins is reported based on their great quenching effect on the blue emission of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). To make a specific determination, a pre-extraction method was also developed using a new nano-sorbent based on the surface-imprinted Zr metal-organic framework on the magnetic graphene oxide (MGO/MOF-808@MIP). The adsorbing efficiency of the prepared composite was remarkably higher than the pristine MOF-808 or bare GO. The presence of GO nanosheets, as well as nanoporous MOF-808 provided a high accessible surface area to form the MIP layer. It provided a great number of MIP sites for high efficient and rapid extraction of aflatoxins. The presence of magnetic nanoparticles in the structure of nanocomposite also facilitated the extraction process using a magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE) system. The combination of this specific and high-performance extraction with simple fluorometric detection caused a potent screening tool for aflatoxins. The method was able to monitor the total aflatoxins content of food samples with a linear range of 0.05–8 ng mL−1, which was more sensitive than the fluorometric system without extraction (5–500 ng mL−1). More developments were made by the application of a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method for the discrimination of the extracted aflatoxins. The system showed high sensitivity and selectivity and was able to detect different aflatoxins with an acceptable resolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxins
Screening tool
GO/ZrMOF composite
MIP-based magnetic extraction
Graphene quantum dots
HPLC

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MOF-808 and its composites
Quenching effect of aflatoxins on the fluorescence emission of GQDs
Application of MGO/MOF-808@MIP for the extraction and preconcentration of aflatoxins
Optimization of the fluorometric detection process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University for its supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic design for the (A) synthesis of MGO/MOF-808@MIP, (B) extraction of aflatoxins, and (C) their determination by GQDs-based fluorometric or HPLC methods.
SEM images for (A) MOF-808, (B) MNPs/MOF-808, (C) MGO, (D) GO/MOF-808, (E) MGO/MOF-808, and (F) MGO/MOF-808@MIP; (G) FTIR and (H) XRD spectra for different synthesized materials.

A) The fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the presence of different aflatoxins (2 ng mL−1) or their mixture (including AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, and AFM1, each one 1 ng mL−1); the insets show the photographic images of corresponding solutions under UV light and the calibration graphs; B and C) calibration graphs for (B) different aflatoxins and (C) their mixture (with equal molar ratios), by GQDs-based fluorometric method after extraction with MGO/MOF-808@MIP.
The chromatogram for the injection of aflatoxins mixture (the concentration of each analyte was 300 ng mL−1), without extraction.

Response of GQDs-based fluorometric method for the determination of some compounds (A) after the extraction process and (B) without extraction process; the inset of (A) shows the results after extraction in the presence of AB1 (2 ng mL−1), [The examined compounds included: AFB1 (aflatoxin B1), OCHA (Ochratoxin A), patulin, ZRLN (Zearalenone), FNB1 (Fumonisin B1), Coum (Coumarin), BisP-A (Bisphenol-A), CPF (Chlorpyrifos), Cyp (Cypermethrin), diazinon, dichlorvos, and malathion, with a concentration of 10 ng mL−1 and glucose, lactose, fructose, sucrose, vitamin C, cysteine, serine, histidine, and urea with a concentration of 2 μg mL−1].
Comparison between the BET surface areas and adsorption capacities of various absorbents

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: These compounds had not magnetic properties or their magnetic features were weak. The separations during the extraction were done by centrifuge.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical figures of merit for the determination of aflatoxins using fluorometric and HPLC methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (n = 5, analyte concentration: 200 ng mL−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (n = 5, analyte concentration: 5 ng mL−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Including equal molar amounts of AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, and AFM1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for the determination of aflatoxins in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The amount of added analytes into the samples before any preparation process (ng g−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The obtained amount of analytes ± standard deviation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The recovery for spiked samples ± relative standard deviation (%).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The obtained amount of analytes ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A simple fluorometric method for rapid screening of aflatoxins after their extraction by magnetic MOF-808/graphene oxide composite and their discrimination by HPLC

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a high-performance screening tool for the selective and sensitive monitoring of aflatoxins is reported based on their great quenching effect on the blue emission of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). To make a specific determination, a pre-extraction method was also developed using a new nano-sorbent based on the surface-imprinted Zr metal-organic framework on the magnetic graphene oxide (MGO/MOF-808@MIP). The adsorbing efficiency of the prepared composite was remarkably higher than the pristine MOF-808 or bare GO. The presence of GO nanosheets, as well as nanoporous MOF-808 provided a high accessible surface area to form the MIP layer. It provided a great number of MIP sites for high efficient and rapid extraction of aflatoxins. The presence of magnetic nanoparticles in the structure of nanocomposite also facilitated the extraction process using a magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE) system. The combination of this specific and high-performance extraction with simple fluorometric detection caused a potent screening tool for aflatoxins. The method was able to monitor the total aflatoxins content of food samples with a linear range of 0.05–8 ng mL−1, which was more sensitive than the fluorometric system without extraction (5–500 ng mL−1). More developments were made by the application of a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method for the discrimination of the extracted aflatoxins. The system showed high sensitivity and selectivity and was able to detect different aflatoxins with an acceptable resolution.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins, as one of the major grain contaminants, are a serious threat to human and animal health. The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations reports that 25% of food products from around the world are significantly contaminated with mycotoxins [1,2]. Among the various mycotoxins, aflatoxins (AF) play an important role in developing countries, leading to destructive diseases [3,4]. They contain furofuran, which causes their toxic and carcinogenic activity. Six aflatoxins including B1, B2, G1, G2, M1, and M2 are considered important. Group B contains a cyclopentane ring, while group G contains a lactone ring, AFM1 and M2 are hydroxyl products of AFB1 and B2, which are mainly present in milk [5]. Aflatoxins are found in a wide range of agricultural products, and the main way people are exposed to them is through diet. Taking large amounts of AF in a short period of time can cause vomiting, abdominal pain, swelling, jaundice, acute liver damage, or even death [6]. Due to their high toxicity and carcinogenicity, strict control over the residues of aflatoxins in food is done in most countries. European Union (EU) sets AFB1 permissible level in food at 2 ng g−1 and the total aflatoxins B1, B2, G1, and G2 at 4 ng g−1 for direct human consumption [7,8]. Currently, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis using C-18 reverse phase columns in conjunction with fluorescence, ultraviolet absorption, or mass spectrometry detectors is the standard method for determining aflatoxin. Also, some biosensors are applied as an initial screening [9–15]. But, most of them are expensive, time-consuming, and tedious, so they cannot be used as a public screening tool. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop fast, robust, and cost-effective alternative technologies for AF monitoring.

On the other hand, the preparation of real samples is one of the most important steps in the analysis of AF and the selection of appropriate extraction method is very important to obtain reliable analysis data. Appropriate sample pretreatment is usually accompanied by preconcentration of the analytes, thus ensuring sufficient sensitivity of the final measurement. The selectivity of the method also increases by eliminating possible interferences in the sample matrix. Among the various extraction techniques, magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE) is recognized for its simplicity, low cost, high preconcentration factor, reusability of adsorbents, and use of small volumes of organic solvents [16]. It is based on the use of a magnetic absorbent, which is dispersed in a large volume of the sample, followed by its recovery using an external magnetic field. MSPE has the necessary ability to overcome the obstacles in conventional extraction methods. In this method, the selection of the adsorbent is an important parameter that can improve the efficiency of the extraction system [16–18]. Several adsorbents have been reported for MSPE, including the magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and composite/hybrid materials containing MNPs [18–20]. These materials usually have a small surface area and require long extraction times due to the low mass transfer of analytes in the solution to the dispersed solid phase.

Recently, much attention has been directed toward the use of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for extraction [17]. MOFs are characterized by the coordinated bonding of metal ions with certain organic linkers, generating a well-organized porous crystalline structure with a regular shape [21,22]. The high porosity, flexible chemical functionalities, and the ability to adjust the pore size make MOFs ideal adsorbents for SPE applications [17,23]. On the other hand, the synergistic effect between highly porous MOFs and MNPs can be exploited to produce high-performance adsorbents for MSPE applications [23–25].

In this study, a MOF-based structure was selected as adsorbents in the extraction and pre-concentration of aflatoxins to provide benefits such as flexibility, high strength, and high adsorption properties. Magnetic graphene oxide/zirconium MOF composite (MGO/MOF-808) was achieved by the solvothermal synthesis of MOF-808 in the presence of MGO. The composite is modified by MNPs to achieve the benefits of magnetic adsorption: good recovery, high efficiency, easy, and economical magnetic separation that eliminates centrifugation and/or filtration steps. The resulting magnetic composite (MGO/MOF-808) was used as a new adsorbent for efficient extraction and preconcentration of aflatoxins using the MSPE procedure. To improve the selectivity of the method, the molecular imprinting technique was also performed on the adsorbent. In the next step, the extracted samples were analyzed by a fast luminescence method, based on the quenching effect of aflatoxins on the fluorescence emission of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Also, the last discrimination of the type of extracted AF was performed by HPLC (Fig. 1 ). To the best of our knowledge, no study has been reported on the application of surface-imprinted magnetic GO/MOF composite for extraction and pretreatment of aflatoxins.

The applied chemicals were obtained in suitable analytical grade, and no additional purification was done before their usage. Aflatoxin stock solutions (10 mg L−1) were prepared in DMF and further dilutions were done by phosphate buffer (pH 7). Other information about the applied material and instruments is provided in “Supporting information”.
The synthesis of GQDs was performed based on the simple pyrolysis of glucose [26]. Details are provided in “Supporting information”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MOF-808 and its composites
ce:section.ce:para: Zr (IV) solution was prepared by adding 970 mg of ZrOCl2⋅8H2O in 50 mL of DMF and FA (1:1 v/v), followed by its sonicating for 30 min. Then, H3BTC (210 mg) was added to the obtained clear solution and the sonication was continued to ensure the complete dissolution of the precursors. The mixture was properly sealed and heated at 120 °C (21 h) and the produced white precipitate was separated by centrifugation for 15 min (12000 rpm). The product (MOF-808) was rinsed with DMF (2 times) and methanol (3 times) and then dried overnight (60 °C) [27].

The same process was applied to synthesize GO/MOF-808 and MGO/MOF-808 composites, except a certain amount of GO or MGO (0.05, 0.1, 0.15, and 0.2 g) was added to the Zr (IV) and H3BTC mixed solution, continued by its sonication for 2 h. MNPs/MOF-808 was also prepared by adding a certain amount of magnetic NPs (50 mg) into the Zr (IV) and H3BTC mixed solution, continued by its sonication for 2 h.
The methods for the preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2 NPs, GO and MGO are provided in “Supporting information”.

Synthesis of the MIP layer onto the nanocomposite was performed using a sol-gel procedure in the presence of DMC as a dummy template [28]. Briefly, 100 mg MGO/MOF-808 (or other composites) was well dispersed in 10 mL of methanol-water (1:1) mixed solvent by its sonication for 30 min. Next, 10 mg DMC and 50 μL APTES were added under continuous agitation and the mixture was stirred for 15 min. Then, 100 μL of TEOS and 0.1 mL of 25% ammonia solution (as the catalyst) were subsequently added and the stirring was continued for 16 h. After completing the polymerization process, the impurities and unreacted reagents were eliminated by washing the produced MIP-capped MGO/MOF-808 with ethanol three times. Afterward, template molecules were removed from the structure of the composite, by dispersing in acetonitrile several times until no template was detected in the eluent by HPLC. Non-imprinted polymer (NIP)-capped composite was also prepared using the same procedure, but in the absence of DMC.

The synthesized MIP-capped MGO/MOF-808 was applied as an efficient adsorbent for the extraction and preconcentration of aflatoxins using the MSPE technique. Briefly, 5 mg of adsorbent, 1 g NaCl and 5 mL phosphate buffer (0.15 M, pH 7) were added to the certain volume (45 mL) of aflatoxin standard or sample solutions, and the mixture was shaken for 10 min to complete the extraction process. Then, the adsorbent was separated by an Nd–B magnet and the supernatant was discarded. The collected adsorbent was washed by its dispersing in deionized water for 10 min. Next, the collected adsorbent was dispersed in 0.5 mL acetonitrile for 5 min to perform the back-extraction process. Finally, 300 μL of the obtained solution was taken for the analysis step.

In order to do the measurement, the extracted analyte solution (300 μL) was added into the mixture of 300 μL phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH 7) and 250 μL GQDs. The final volume of the mixture was increased to 3 mL by deionized water, and the emission signal of GQDs was recorded at 455 nm using the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

The chromatography analyses were done using a high-performance chromatography device (KNAUER S-2850, Germany), with a photodiode array detector and stationary phase of C18 (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 μm). Methanol and deionized water (65:35 v/v) mixed solvent was used as the mobile phase, with isocratic elution at a flow rate of 1.2 mL min−1. The column temperature was 40 °C and the injection volume was 25 μL. In this condition, the retention times for AFM2, AFM1, AFG2, AFG1, AFB2, and AFB1 were 3.82, 4.37, 4.78, 5.62, 9.23, and 11.37 min, respectively. Detection was carried out at 365 nm, and the areas under the chromatogram peaks obtained at the optimal condition were used as analytical signals.

Different food products were selected as real samples to assess their aflatoxin contents, using the developed method. For rice and wheat flour samples, a 5 g homogeneous powdered sample was dispersed in 10 mL methanol/water (70:30) solvent and sonicated for 20 min to completely extract analytes. The mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was to 45 mL by water.

Preparation of milk sample (20 mL) was performed by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 15 min) to separate the fat, followed by its filtration through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. It was diluted with 25 mL deionized water before the extraction process.

TEM image of GQDs indicated their homogeneous dispersion with an average size of about 6 nm (Fig. S1A). Besides, the XRD spectrum displayed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ values of 12–25° (Fig. S1B), revealing the graphitic structure of GQDs. The assessment of interlayer distance of GQDs showed a value of 4.05 Å, which is similar to reported studies [29]. This value is slightly larger than the basal plane of graphene, due to the presence of some oxygen functional groups. Further, the creation of a broad XRD peak for GQDs exhibits their small size. FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs is also shown in Fig. S1C, which proves the existence of some functional groups in their structure. The absorption peaks at 1622 and 1743 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of CC and CO, respectively. The peaks at 3200-3600 cm−1 and 1041 cm−1 can be linked to the existence of O–H groups and C–O bonds, respectively. Finally, the peaks at 1356 and 2924 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of C–H bonds.

The synthesized GQDs showed two characteristic absorption peaks around 249 and 304 nm, which are related to the π–π* transitions of CC groups in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and n–π* transitions of CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S1D). Besides, the prepared GQDs exhibited a high fluorescence emission with clear dependency on the excitation wavelength (Fig. S1E). As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence emission was shifted to the higher wavelengths, and the maximum emission intensity was obtained on 460 nm (λex = 365 nm) with a quantum yield of 29.3% (using rhodamine B as reference material).

Synthesis of adsorbent was based on the polymerization reaction of Zr(IV) and H3BDC in the presence of MGO; the interactions between Zr cations and MGO carboxylic functional groups, or interactions of benzene rings of H3BDC ligands with GO can result in the fabrication of a MOF layer on the surface of MGO. Therefore, the product is the Zr-based MOF grown on the surface of magnetic graphene oxide nanosheets (defined as MGO/MOF-808). Besides, the surface imprinting process was performed by self-assembly reaction of APTES and TEOS monomers in the presence of DMC, as the common dummy template. This interaction of DMC molecules with the amino groups of APTES results in the trapping of DMC molecules into the formed polymeric matrix. The process was done in the presence of MOF-88/MGO, as a support for the imprinted polymer. The produced composite contained MOF-88/MGO covered by a molecularly imprinted polymer layer (defined as MGO/MOF-808@MIP). It was eluted several times by acetonitrile to effectively eliminate DMC molecules (controlled by HPLC), leaving some unoccupied sites on the surface of composites. DMC is easily soluble in acetonitrile and can be efficiently removed from MIP sites. The created free MIP sites are matched to the aflatoxin molecules and can specifically adsorb them from a complex matrix. Fig. 1A shows a summarized preparation process to obtain MGO/MOF-808.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the prepared nanomaterials. The regular octahedral morphology with a size of less than 400 nm for MOF-808 is clear in Fig. 2 A [27]. The synthesis of MOF-808 in the presence of MNPs also led to the production of the same morphology (Fig. 2B). The obtained MNPs/MOF-808 showed great magnetic properties (Fig S2A). This shows the embedding of Fe3O4@SiO2 NPs with an average size of 25 nm (Fig. S2B) inside the structure of MOF-808. MGO/MOF-808 also well kept the magnetic properties and can be easily collected from its dispersed solution using a magnet (Fig. S2C). The attachment of MNPs on the surface of GO is clear in the SEM image (Fig. 2C) The SEM images for GO/MOF-808 and MGO/MOF-808 showed the MOF-808 crystal grown on the GO or MGO surface (Fig. 2D and E). Exposing GO (or MGO) to the MOF synthesis precursors led to a clear increase in its thickness compared to bare GO (Fig. S2D). Besides, the presence of spherical magnetic NPs in the structure of composite is also obvious. Finally, the presence of a rough MIP layer covering the nanocomposite surface can be easily observed in the SEM image of MGO/MOF-808@MIP (Fig. 2F), demonstrating its suitable synthesis.

Fig. 2G and S3 show the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the obtained nanomaterials. The pristine MOF-808 showed its characteristic peaks at about 658 and 758 cm−1, assigned to the presence of Zr–O bonds. Also, the peak at 1380 cm−1 is related to the Zr–O–H groups. The O–CO functional groups caused the absorption peaks at about 1575 and 1618 cm−1 (Fig. 2G, spectrum a). The appearance of absorption bands at 598 and 1182 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of MNPs/MOF-808 can demonstrate the presence of magnetic NPs (Fig. 2G, spectrum b). These peaks are ascribed to Fe–O and Si–O bonds, respectively (Fig. S3). Besides, the FTIR spectrum for MGO/MOF-808 (Fig. 2G, spectrum c) is similar to magnetic MOF, with a few modifications at about 1050, 1248, and 1678 cm−1, which can be related to the C–O and CO vibrations in GO nanosheets (Fig. S3). Eventually, the main change in the FTIR spectrum of MIP-modified composite includes the presence of a weak peak at 3524 cm−1, which can be assigned to –NH2 groups of APTES in the MIP layer (Fig. 2G, spectrum d). Also, a clear change was occurred at about 1128 cm−1, related to the Si–O bonds in the created MIP layer. The FTIR spectra of produced MGO/MOF-808@MIP before and after the elimination of DMC template molecules also showed a shift in the absorption peak at 3524 cm−1 to higher wavenumbers (Spectra are not presented). It can be related to free amine groups of APTES after eliminating DMC, demonstrating their interaction during the synthesis of the MIP layer.

The results for X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies are shown in Fig. 2H. A broad diffraction peak at about 14° for GO is related to its amorphous and planar structure (curve a). The XRD pattern of Fe3O4 magnetic NPs also contained specific peaks at 2θ values of about 17.8° (111), 29.7° (220), 35.0° (311), 42.5° (400), 52.8° (422), 56.6° (511), 62.4° (440), 70.9° (620), and 73.8° (553) (curve b). After coating with silicate layer, the broad peak of Si–O bonds appeared at 22° (curve c) [30,31]. Besides, the pristine MOF-808 showed its unique diffraction peaks at 8.32° (311) and 8.69° (322) (curve d), which is in good agreement with the literature [27,31]. MNPs/MOF-808 and MGO/MOF-808 also showed the characteristic diffractions peaks for magnetic NPs and MOF-808, demonstrating their accurate formation (curves e and f). Finally, in the XRD spectrum of MGO/MOF-808@MIP, the peaks for MOF-808 and magnetic NPs appeared with relatively weak intensities, which can be related to the presence of amorphous MIP layer on the composite (curve g).

The results for N2 adsorption/desorption experiments are also summarized in Table 1 . The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of pristine MOF-808 was 1293.4 m2 g−1, representing its great potential for adsorption and extraction applications. The modification of MOF with MNPs decreases its specific surface area; the higher amount of MNPs applied in the synthesis of MNPs/MOF-808, the lower BET surface area of resulted composite. However, the presence of MNPs is beneficial for easy separation of the adsorbent from the sample solution. Besides, growing MOF-808 on the surface of MGO nanosheets resulted in a relatively lower surface area. However, it improved the adsorbing properties of the final MIP-capped nanocomposite. GO nanosheets cause MOF crystals to grow on their surface, so more surface area is accessible for MIP layer formation. In this condition, the number of created MIP sites is increased, improving the adsorbing capacity. As can be seen, utilization of a higher amount of MGO (>0.15 g) in the synthesis process remarkably decreases the BET surface area of the produced MGO/MOF-808, and so, the adsorbing features of the composite are weakened. Thus, the synthesis of composite for the extraction aims was done using in the presence of 0.15 g of MGO. Also, the best results were obtained by using 50 mg of magnetic NPs applied to the synthesis of MGO (Table 1). Lower amounts of magnetic NPs resulted in the poor magnetic features of the final nanocomposite, while the higher amounts than 50 mg caused a remarkable decrease in BET surface area of obtained MGO/MOF-808, decreasing its adsorbing capacity. For MGO/MOF-808@MIP, the BET surface area decreased remarkably (569.1 m2 g−1), but the presence of a high number of specific MIP sites for the analyte molecules led to a highly selective extraction with high efficiency. In addition, the BET surface area of MGO/MOF-808@MIP is higher than the NIP-capped MGO/MOF-808 composite (427.3 m2 g−1) that can be related to the presence of MIP empty sites in the former composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of aflatoxins on the fluorescence emission of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The initial experiments showed that aflatoxins can effectively quench the fluorescence emission of GQDs. Fig. 3 A shows the fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the presence of different aflatoxins or their mixture. The blue emission of GQDs occurs at 460 nm, and its intensity is decreased in the presence of different aflatoxins (Inset of Fig. 3A). This effect can be related to the interaction of aflatoxins with functional groups on the surface of GQDs, which can alter its optical properties. Aflatoxins contain carbonyl groups and aromatic rings, and so can interact with the surface of GQDs via hydrogen bonding or π-π interactions. Fig. 3A also shows the linear dependency of emission intensity to the concentration of aflatoxins in the range of 5–500 ng mL−1, with detection limits in the range of 1.78–1.95 ng mL−1. This relationship is useful to design a simple probe for the determination of total aflatoxins. In order to make a selective measurement tool, a pre-extraction method was also applied for the extraction of aflatoxins from complex matrices before their determination by the designed fluorometric system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MGO/MOF-808@MIP for the extraction and preconcentration of aflatoxins
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized MGO/MOF-808@MIP showed a high adsorption capacity for the extraction of aflatoxins. Initial experiments were related to the comparison of the adsorption features of prepared nanocomposite and its components. Each adsorbent was applied to the extraction of AFB1 from its solution (5 ng mL−1) and after the desorption step in acetonitrile, the amount of extracted analyte was evaluated by their effect on the fluorescence emission of GQDs. It should be mentioned that the reported results for the applied adsorbents were in their optimum condition (Table 1). As can be seen, MGO/MOF-808@MIP showed high efficiency to adsorb AFB1. The performance of this nanocomposite is slightly lower than that of GO/MOF-808@MIP. The reduced surface of nano-sorbents in the presence of MNPs (Table 1) can be probably responsible for this result, however, the modification of adsorbents with MNPs is useful to obtain a simple separation process. Although the surface area of MOF-808 is decreased by their synthesis in the presence of GO or MGO (Table 1), the adsorption efficiencies of MGO/MOF-808@MIP and GO/MOF-808@MIP were greatly higher compared to the pristine MOF-808. The presence of GO nanosheets in the structure of nanocomposite provides more surface area accessible for the formation of the MIP layer, and so, more specific sites are created for the extraction of aflatoxins. On the other hand, the adsorptive capacity of MGO/MOF-808@MIP was better than that of MIP-capped magnetic carbon nanotubes/MOF-808 composite (MCNTs/MOF-808@MIP). This effect can be described based on the higher surface area of MGO/MOF-808@MIP. MOF prepared on the surface of GO nanosheets can provide a larger surface area for the MIP layer.

In this step, the capacity of prepared MGO/MOF-808@MIP was investigated using different initial concentrations of AFB1 (C0) (Fig. S4A). As the C0 increased the adsorbed amount (Q) was increased and a maximum absorption (19.8 mg g−1) was obtained by C0 = 20 mg L−1, representing the high tendency of applied nano-sorbent toward the AFB1. In addition, higher concentrations of analyte caused higher initial adsorption rates. The equilibrium conditions were also achieved after about 10 min (Fig. S4B), showing a rapid desorption process. To recognize the mechanism of adsorption, Langmuir (1/Q-1/Ct) and Freundlich's (Ln(Q)-Ln(Ct)) adsorption isotherm models were investigated. The obtained results are well fitted to the Langmuir model. In other words, aflatoxin molecules are adsorbed as uniform monolayers on the surface of the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fluorometric detection process
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a higher sensitivity, the conditions for the fluorometric determination of aflatoxins were optimized. The experiments were done using AFB1 (300 ng mL−1), as the model analyte, without any pre-concentration step. The results showed that the best analytical signals (F0–F) were obtained using 250 μL of GQDs solution mixed with 300 μL of analyte solution and adjusted at pH 7–8, using phosphate buffer (0.02 M) (Fig. S5). The high acidic or basic media caused a decreased signal, probably due to the changes in the functional groups on the GQDs that can affect the interaction of aflatoxin molecules with their surface (Fig. S5A). The concentration of phosphate buffer in the range of 0.005–0.025 M did not change the analytical signal, notably. The other used buffers, like Tris and ammonium acetate, remarkably decreased the sensitivity (Fig. S5B). In addition, higher amounts of GQDs than 250 μL led to an overload emission for blank solution (F0) (Fig. S5C).

To achieve maximum extraction efficiency, the effects of some key parameters on the performance of adsorbent were studied. The experiments were done by dispersing the MGO/MOF-808@MIP adsorbent into the solution of AFB1 with a constant concentration (5 ng mL−1), followed by a back-extraction step. The extracted analyte was evaluated by its quenching effect on the emission of GQDs, and the decreased fluorescence intensity (F0–F) was considered as the analytical signal (Fig. S6). The obtained optimal conditions included: phosphate buffer (0.015 M and pH 6–8), 5 mg of MGO/MOF-808@MIP, extraction time of 10 min, and extraction volume of 50 mL. The more acidic (pH < 6) or basic (pH > 8) conditions caused a decrease in the extraction efficiency, due to the modification of functional groups of MIP sites and affecting their interactions with the analyte (Fig. S6A). The best condition was obtained using phosphate buffer with a concentration of 0.015 M (Fig. S6B). Besides, the effects of different adsorbents modified by the MIP layer were investigated on the extraction results and maximum efficiency was obtained by 5 mg of MGO/MOF-808@MIP. Higher amounts of adsorbent did not provide any further enhancements in the extraction results (Fig. S6C). Also, the best results were obtained by dispersing adsorbent in 50 mL of extraction solution within 10 min (Fig. S6D and S6E). Moreover, NaCl was used to adjust the ionic strength and the maximum performance of the adsorbent was obtained in the presence of 0.5 M NaCl. However, the higher amounts of NaCl led to a declined sensitivity (Fig. S6F).

The optimization experiments were also continued for the desorption process and the best results were obtained by dispersing of adsorbent in 0.5 mL acetonitrile for 5 min (Fig. S7A and S7B). Other solvents showed poor efficiencies to desorb analyte molecules from the MIP sites Also, no improvement in the desorption results was obtained by adding acid (1–5%) in acetonitrile.

The application of high-performance adsorbent based on the MOF and GO nanosheets composite for the MIP-based pre-extraction of aflatoxins coupled with a rapid and efficient fluorometric detection system can cause a valuable screening tool for on-site monitoring purposes. The measurement of the total aflatoxin content of samples was carried out in two steps. First, MGO/MOF-808@MIP was applied for their extraction from sample solutions. Then, the extracted analytes were monitored by the fluorometric detection system. The decreasing effects (F0–F) of aflatoxins on the emission GQDs, as well as calibration graphs for the determination of aflatoxins without or with the pre-extraction process, are shown in Fig. 3. The obtained figures of merits are also summarized in Table 2 . The results showed that the pre-extraction step improves the sensitivity by about 90 times. It means that the preconcentration of aflatoxins occurred during the extraction. An enrichment factor (EF = m’/m) of 92.7 was obtained for the determination of total aflatoxins using the ratio of calibration graph slopes with (m’, Fig. 3C) and without preconcentration step (m, inset of Fig. 3A). Also, the precision of the developed method was evaluated using the repeated measurements of aflatoxins with a constant concentration, which resulted in acceptable RSD% values (Table 2).

Also, the results for the determination of aflatoxins by the fluorometric system after a pre-extraction using MGO/MOF-808@NIP showed a relatively lower sensitivity, confirming the key role of MIP sites on the MGO/MOF-808@MIP in the extraction of aflatoxins (Table 2).

HPLC method was also used to detect different types of aflatoxins. Fig. 4 shows the chromatogram for the injection of the mixed solution of 6 different aflatoxins (without extraction). An acceptable resolution was obtained in optimum condition between the resulted peaks and each aflatoxin can be measured individually. Table 2 shows the obtained analytical figures of merit. The preconcentration step before the HPLC analysis caused an effective enhancement in the sensitivity of aflatoxin determination. For example, the linear range for the measurement of AFB1 was 0.03–5 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.009 ng mL−1 (obtained by the signal-to-noise ratio (3:1) of samples with a low concentration of analyte).

A comparison between the obtained figures of merit and the results of some other reported methods for the determination of aflatoxins is presented in Table S1. Despite its high simplicity, the designed method provides high sensitive determination system. The obtained detection limits are comparable with reported methods in the literature. Besides, the promising point of the presented work is the combination of a rapid screening probe for aflatoxins using the simple fluorometric method with an HPLC-based endorsing technique. The suggested extraction system is compatible with both fluorometric and HPLC methods. Besides, MIP-based extraction remarkably improved the selectivity of the method. Therefore, the development of such combined assays for the reliable detection of aflatoxins is of great importance.

The selectivity of the developed detection system was studied by extraction and measurement of aflatoxin AFB1 (5 ng mL−1) in the absence and presence of some other compounds (Fig. 5 A). The results confirmed the adequate selectivity of the design fluorometric method toward the determination of AFB1 after its extraction with the MIP-based adsorbent. However, most of the examined compounds showed high interfering effects on the detection of aflatoxins without pre-extraction step (Fig. 5B). These results demonstrated the key role of MGO/MOF-808@MIP to obtain a high specific detection method.

The stability of MGO/MOF-808@MIP was investigated by its exposure to aqueous media (pH 7) for certain times. Then, it was applied for the extraction of AFB1, followed by the fluorometric detection method. The results showed that the adsorbent is completely stable for at least 30 days (Fig. S8A). Also, some cyclic extraction experiments were done to study the reusability of the MGO/MOF-808@MIP (Fig. S8B). The efficiency of the adsorbent decreased to 96.1% and 89.8%, after eight and ten cyclic applications, respectively. Thus, the newly designed composite had good stability and was applicable for at least 8 successive extractions, without a notable decrease in its performance.

Finally, the stability of GQDs was investigated after its preservation at 4 °C for different times. The results confirmed a constant fluorescence emission after 3 months, confirming the high stability of GQDs.

The developed analytical method was applied to measure the aflatoxins in some food samples. The preparation process of real samples is presented in “Supporting information”. The results were in good agreement with data obtained by an official HPLC tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) method (Supporting information), confirming the acceptable accuracy of the presented method (Table 3 ). Besides, it can be seen that the total amount of aflatoxins detected by fluorescence assay is almost equal to the sum of the concentrations of individual aflatoxins detected by HPLC. This result can verify the suitability of fluorescence assay as a screening method. The validation of data was also investigated by spiking certain amounts of aflatoxins solution into the samples before any preparations. The samples were extracted and analyzed by the developed method (Table 3).

The synthesis of MOF-808 was successfully performed on the magnetic GO and the characterization analyses confirmed the construction of MGO/MOF-808. The prepared nanocomposite indicated a superior surface area for the formation of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The synthesis of the MIP layer on the MGO/MOF-808 was done in the presence of DMC as the template and examined for the extraction of aflatoxins. The obtained MGO/MOF-808@MIP showed great efficiency for the specific extraction of aflatoxins. The extracted analytes were analyzed by a fluorometric detection method, based on their quenching effects on the blue emission of GQDs. This technique provided a potent, rapid, and sensitive screening tool to monitor the total aflatoxin content of real samples with a linear range of 0.05–8 ng mL−1. Performing a pre-extraction before the analysis of aflatoxins caused a great enhancement in the sensitivity by 92.7 times. Besides, the analyses were followed by discrimination and measurement of different types of aflatoxins using the HPLC method. Thus, the presented work includes a full analytical system for the initial screening of samples for their aflatoxin content using the rapid fluorometric system, followed by validating the selected samples by the HPLC method. The applicability of the method was examined by monitoring aflatoxins in food samples as well.

Sevda Alilou: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft. Maliheh Amirzehni: Supervision, Conceptualization, Investigation, Parvin Alizadeh Eslami: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing.
